model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model targeting a compact, high-signal set of five operationalized workplace-issue criteria (C01–C05) and seven modifiable predictors (P01–P07) that align with the self-perpetuating cycle: errors trigger threat appraisals, leading to compensatory overwork and fatigue, which worsens performance. The predictor set emphasizes sleep/energy management, task-preparation routines, boundary setting, cognitive offloading, threat-reappraisal, and social support, to interrupt the cycle with momentary variability. Data collection leans on frequent EMA prompts (4 per day) plus a concise daily diary, with passive sleep proxies where feasible, designed for straightforward gVAR estimation over a two-week window.
variable_selection_notes: Variables chosen to (1) preserve the complaint's resolution (C01–C05) while (2) minimizing redundancy from overlapping self-regulation constructs, and (3) ensuring substantial within-person variance at the planned sampling rate. Sleep/fatigue are primary drivers of cognitive lapses and fatigue-related errors; pre-task checklists and boundary-setting provide concrete, actionable levers; cognitive offloading and threat-reappraisal modulate cognitive load and catastrophic worry; social support buffers stress. Assumptions: predictors are moderately malleable in short-to-medium term; likelihood of nonlinear effects is acknowledged where indicated (set in edges/predictor-criterion mapping). Bias/uncertainty: measurement error may vary across predictors (self-report bias for fatigue vs. objective sleep proxies); plan includes z-scoring within-person transformations for VAR stability.

momentary_ema.notes: 4 prompts/day across typical work hours; jitter to reduce response fatigue

daily_diary.notes: End-of-day brief reflective item on overall workload and fatigue

passive_streams.notes: Sleep/wake proxies (if available) and activity metrics from wearables; treat as supplementary signal

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Feasible with clear EMA prompts and minimal participant burden; good initial data density for gVAR estimation
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Sleep/energy, task preparation, and perceived support expected to vary within-day and across days, supporting within-person variance required for gVAR
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Moderate risk due to daily prompts; mitigate with reminders and optional flexible completion windows

safety_notes: Not diagnosing or prescribing; this plan does not imply treatment. If any participant reports acute risk (self-harm, severe distress), escalate to appropriate clinical support per protocol.

measurement.item_or_signal: Please rate the recent rate/likelihood of small mistakes on a 1 (none) to 9 (frequent) scale for current shift/work period.

The closest, most specific CRITERION leaf is 'careless_errors_in_tasks' (idx 28040), which operationalizes frequency of careless mistakes in tasks. The measurement mode (momentary self-report via smartphone EMA) matches the leaf's intended use. This leaf provides a direct match to an error-rate construct within workplace tasks, aligning with the pseudoprofile's C01 objective and the proposed mechanism (fatigue/attention lapses leading to errors) without overfitting to unrelated sleep or cognitive-architecture items.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Regular sleep supports cognitive control and reduces fatigue-driven errors.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Maintain consistent bed/wake times; optimize sleep environment; limit late caffeine.

measurement.item_or_signal: Please rate your sleep quality last night and the consistency of your sleep schedule on a 1-9 scale (1 = very poor/very inconsistent, 9 = excellent/very consistent).

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces daytime errors via improved attention and speed.
Stabler sleep reduces anxiety about performance.
Restorative sleep lowers fatigue later in the day.
Sleep quality improves immediate cognitive performance.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: P01 maps to Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene. The best leaf is the BIO leaf ' Sleep_quality_rating' under daily_and_retrospective_logs, which matches the provided measurement item: 'Please rate your sleep quality last night and the consistency of your sleep schedule on a 1-9 scale' with four EMA prompts per day over past 24 hours. This leaf provides a direct, operational self-report signal of sleep quality and schedule consistency (core components of sleep hygiene) and aligns with the pseudoprofile's EMA-based data collection for gVAR estimation. Compared to other leaves (e.g., sleep diaries, presleep routines, or generic sleep questionnaires), this one exactly captures the composite sleep quality/consistency signal described in the variable, making it the most specific and appropriate mapping.

predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Plan 2–4 minute microbreaks after blocks of 25–50 minutes; light movement; hydration.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your energy level on a 1-9 scale (1 = very low energy, 9 = high energy).
measurement.notes: Captures current energy state for fatigue management.

expected_effects.rationale: Lower fatigue should reduce daytime tiredness.
Higher energy improves sustained attention.
Stable energy supports fewer minor errors.

feasibility.notes: Low burden; aligns with work blocks

The mechanism aligns with energy management to reduce fatigue and maintain attention, and the microbreaks concept represents an actionable energy-lever within Sleep_Routines_and_Practices/biological self-reports domain. measurement.item_or_signal: Please rate the recent rate/likelihood of small mistakes on a 1 (none) to 9 (frequent) scale for current shift/work period.

criteria.label: Catastrophic job-loss expectation (anticipatory fear)
criteria.query_text_used: Catastrophic job-loss expectation (anticipatory fear) | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate your thoughts/feelings that your mistakes could imminently lead to being fired, on a 1 (not at all) to 9 (extremely) scale. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Captures threat appraisal intensity/frequency.

mapping.rationale: variable maps to anticipatory threat appraisal about a potentially catastrophic work outcome (being fired due to mistakes). The closest leaf is fear_that_something_awful_may_happen under clinical anxiety constructs; measurement item explicitly assesses imminent firing threat on EMA scale, capturing threat appraisal mechanism and short recall window.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your thoughts/feelings that your mistakes could imminently lead to being fired, on a 1 (not at all) to 9 (extremely) scale.

criteria.label: Compensatory overwork (working late)
criteria.query_text_used: Compensatory overwork (working late) | Operational item/signal: Since your last mistake, how much time have you spent working beyond scheduled hours (overtime) and/or skipping breaks? Rate 1 (none) to 9 (extensive) on current episode. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Captures overt task-sustaining behavior.

mapping.rationale: The variable C03 describes compensatory overwork (working late) and an EMA item capturing time spent working beyond scheduled hours (1-9) aligns directly with the leaf 'excessive_work_hours_persisting_outside_expectations' under work addiction/workaholism. This leaf represents a concrete behavioral pattern (overtime outside expected norms) that matches the intended operational construct. The measurement mode (EMA_self_report via smartphone) and momentary assessment (past_6_hours) fit the operational scope, providing a precise, episode-level signal of overtime behavior that the gVAR model aims to capture. While other leaves address related constructs (e.g., preoccupation, persistence, impairment), this one is the most specific for overtime in the current episode.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since your last mistake, how much time have you spent working beyond scheduled hours (overtime) and/or skipping breaks? Rate 1 (none) to 9 (extensive) on current episode.

criteria.label: Daytime tiredness/fatigue
criteria.query_text_used: Daytime tiredness/fatigue | Operational item/signal: Right now, rate your energy/restoration level during work on a 1 (very low) to 9 (very high) scale. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Fatigue level related to extended work hours.

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 (Daytime tiredness/fatigue) maps to the leaf 'daytime_fatigue_intensity' (clinical sleep-wake disorders > daytime fatigue intensity). The measurement is EMA_self_report via smartphone, momentary assessment with a 1–9 scale of energy/fatigue during work, 4 prompts per day, past 6 hours. This leaf provides the most specific operational construct for daytime fatigue intensity aligned with the stated measurement item and signal, beyond more general fatigue or fatigue-related constructs.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your energy/restoration level during work on a 1 (very low) to 9 (very high) scale.
measurement.notes: Fatigue level related to extended work hours.

criteria.label: Attention/cognitive performance decline linked to fatigue
criteria.query_text_used: Attention/cognitive performance decline linked to fatigue | Operational item/signal: While fatigued, rate the frequency of attention lapses or processing slowdowns on a 1 (none) to 9 (frequent) scale. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Captures fatigue-related cognitive vulnerability.

mapping.rationale: The variable C05 targets fatigue-related attention/cognitive decline. The best-matching leaf is cognitive_fatigue (idx 14541), which explicitly encodes cognitive fatigue as a nervous-system disorder-related concept. The measurement item asks participants to rate fatigue-related attention lapses or processing slowdowns on a 1-9 scale during EMA prompts, aligning directly with cognitive_fatigue as the mechanism driving attentional vulnerability. This provides the most specific operational construct for the described EMA_self_report measurement, within the pseudoprofile’s focus on fatigue as a primary driver of cognitive lapses.

source_variable.label: Attention/cognitive performance decline linked to fatigue

measurement.item_or_signal: While fatigued, rate the frequency of attention lapses or processing slowdowns on a 1 (none) to 9 (frequent) scale.

predictors.label: Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Regular sleep supports cognitive control and reduces fatigue-driven errors. | Intervention examples: Maintain consistent bed/wake times; optimize sleep environment; limit late caffeine. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Please rate your sleep quality last night and the consistency of your sleep schedule on a 1-9 scale (1 = very poor/very inconsistent, 9 = excellent/very consistent). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: P01 maps to Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene. The best leaf is the BIO leaf ' Sleep_quality_rating' under daily_and_retrospective_logs, which matches the provided measurement item: 'Please rate your sleep quality last night and the consistency of your sleep schedule on a 1-9 scale' with four EMA prompts per day over past 24 hours. This leaf provides a direct, operational self-report signal of sleep quality and schedule consistency (core components of sleep hygiene) and aligns with the pseudoprofile's EMA-based data collection for gVAR estimation. Compared to other leaves (e.g., sleep diaries, presleep routines, or generic sleep questionnaires), this one exactly captures the composite sleep quality/consistency signal described in the variable, making it the most specific and appropriate mapping.

source_variable.label: Sleep regularization and sleep hygiene
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Regular sleep supports cognitive control and reduces fatigue-driven errors.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Maintain consistent bed/wake times; optimize sleep environment; limit late caffeine.

measurement.item_or_signal: Please rate your sleep quality last night and the consistency of your sleep schedule on a 1-9 scale (1 = very poor/very inconsistent, 9 = excellent/very consistent).

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces daytime errors via improved attention and speed.
Stabler sleep reduces anxiety about performance.
Restorative sleep lowers fatigue later in the day.
Sleep quality improves immediate cognitive performance.

predictors.label: Structured fatigue-energy management with planned microbreaks
predictors.query_text_used: Structured fatigue-energy management with planned microbreaks | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Energy management reduces fatigue and maintains attention. | Intervention examples: Plan 2–4 minute microbreaks after blocks of 25–50 minutes; light movement; hydration. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now, rate your energy level on a 1-9 scale (1 = very low energy, 9 = high energy). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is 4525 (mental_energy_rating) because var P02 is a momentary energy/fatigue state captured via EMA self-report with a 1-9 energy rating. The measurement item matches the variable’s prompt (rate your energy now). The mechanism aligns with energy management to reduce fatigue and maintain attention, and the microbreaks concept represents an actionable energy-lever within Sleep_Routines_and_Practices/biological self-reports domain. This is the most specific, closest operational construct among candidates given the variable label, measurement approach, and intended fatigue-energy management function.

source_variable.label: Structured fatigue-energy management with planned microbreaks
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Energy management reduces fatigue and maintains attention.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Plan 2–4 minute microbreaks after blocks of 25–50 minutes; light movement; hydration.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your energy level on a 1-9 scale (1 = very low energy, 9 = high energy).
measurement.notes: Captures current energy state for fatigue management.

expected_effects.rationale: Lower fatigue should reduce daytime tiredness.
Higher energy improves sustained attention.
Stable energy supports fewer minor errors.

feasibility.notes: Low burden; aligns with work blocks

predictors.label: Pre-task checklists and error-proofing routines
predictors.query_text_used: Pre-task checklists and error-proofing routines | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Richen routines before tasks reduces error likelihood. | Intervention examples: Brief checklist before task start; stop-and-check steps; cognitive forcing functions. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Before starting your last task, did you complete a brief pre-task checklist? Rate its thoroughness on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Behavior_Change_and_Activation

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf 2057 corresponds to PSYCHO / cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation / cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids / external_memory_systems / design_and_use / checklists_for_error_prevention, which directly matches the variable 'Pre-task checklists and error-proofing routines.' The EMA item (Before starting your last task, did you complete a brief pre-task checklist? rated 1-9) captures momentary adherence to a pre-task checklist, aligning with the mechanism that Richen routines before tasks reduces error likelihood. The pseudoprofile notes emphasize pre-task checklists as actionable levers, supporting this mapping over more generalized or presleep/other cognitive strategies.

source_variable.label: Pre-task checklists and error-proofing routines
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Richen routines before tasks reduces error likelihood.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief checklist before task start; stop-and-check steps; cognitive forcing functions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Before starting your last task, did you complete a brief pre-task checklist? Rate its thoroughness on a 1-9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Preparation lowers chance of subsequent errors.
Planning reduces distraction and lapses.

feasibility.notes: Moderate cognitive load per prompt; keep brief

predictors.label: Boundary-setting and supervisor alignment to cap overtime
predictors.query_text_used: Boundary-setting and supervisor alignment to cap overtime | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Clear boundaries reduce fatigue accumulation and threat exposure. | Intervention examples: Set end-of-day cutoff; align with supervisor; use auto-logout reminders. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how effectively did you set and maintain boundaries to limit overtime? Rate on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > change_process_and_therapeutic_phases > behavioral_integration > lifestyle_performance_and_wellbeing_integration

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / lifestyle_performance_and_wellbeing_integration / stress_load_boundary_setting_practice. It precisely encodes boundary-setting to cap overtime, aligning with the var label (Boundary-setting and supervisor alignment to cap overtime) and the EMA self-report item (Today, how effectively did you set and maintain boundaries to limit overtime? on a 1-9 scale). The measurement context (4 prompts/day, past 24 hours) fits the intended data collection for boundary management as an actionable lever to reduce fatigue and threat exposure. Mechanism rationale fits the PSYCHO domain's focus on boundary-setting to modulate stress load and performance.

source_variable.label: Boundary-setting and supervisor alignment to cap overtime
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Clear boundaries reduce fatigue accumulation and threat exposure.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Set end-of-day cutoff; align with supervisor; use auto-logout reminders.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how effectively did you set and maintain boundaries to limit overtime? Rate on a 1-9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Less overtime reduces threat appraisal.
Less fatigue accumulation after late work.

feasibility.notes: Requires coordination with supervisor; feasible with protocol

predictors.label: External cognitive offloading tools and prompts
predictors.query_text_used: External cognitive offloading tools and prompts | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Offloading reduces cognitive load, improving attention and reducing errors. | Intervention examples: Use checklists, note-taking templates, and other offloading aids. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Have you used an external tool (e.g., checklist, notes) to offload cognitive load in the last task? Rate usefulness on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation > cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: PSYCHO / cognitive_capacity_enhancement_and_stimulation / cognitive_offloading_and_external_aids / external_memory_systems / design_and_use / external_aids_use_training. The variable External cognitive offloading tools and prompts aligns with external memory systems design and use (offloading cognitive load via checklists, prompts). Measurement modality EMA_self_report via smartphone app (momentary) assessing usage and perceived usefulness fits the design_and_use subnode; mechanism: offloading reduces cognitive load and errors, aligning with the predictor's aim to provide tangible cognitive aids to manage task-related load.

source_variable.label: External cognitive offloading tools and prompts
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Offloading reduces cognitive load, improving attention and reducing errors.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Use checklists, note-taking templates, and other offloading aids.

measurement.item_or_signal: Have you used an external tool (e.g., checklist, notes) to offload cognitive load in the last task? Rate usefulness on a 1-9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Offloading reduces error propensity.
Less cognitive load supports attention and processing.
Better cognitive management reduces fatigue impact.

predictors.label: Cognitive restructuring and resilience training (threat appraisal)
predictors.query_text_used: Cognitive restructuring and resilience training (threat appraisal) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Change maladaptive threat beliefs to reduce catastrophic response. | Intervention examples: CBT-based reframing exercises; adaptive coping scripts. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last few hours, rate how strongly you believe your mistakes will lead to firing on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Metacognitive_and_Attention_based_Interventions

mapping.rationale: The variable P06 targets Cognitive restructuring and resilience training focused on threat appraisal. Among candidates, idx 4162 maps to PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe, which precisely embodies reframing maladaptive threat beliefs (threat appraisal) via cognitive restructuring and reappraisal. Measurement is EMA_self_report via a momentary assessment of belief about consequences (rate mistakes leading to firing) consistent with threat appraisal monitoring. This leaf is the most specific operational construct aligning label, mechanism (threat appraisal reframing), and measurement context in the provided ontology options.

source_variable.label: Cognitive restructuring and resilience training (threat appraisal)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Change maladaptive threat beliefs to reduce catastrophic response.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: CBT-based reframing exercises; adaptive coping scripts.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last few hours, rate how strongly you believe your mistakes will lead to firing on a 1-9 scale.

expected_effects.rationale: Less threat appraisals reduces panic after errors.
Improved coping reduces attention lapses under fatigue.
Less threat lowers error propensity via improved performance.

feasibility.notes: Engagement with CBT-like prompts; feasible with brief prompts

predictors.label: Structured social support and supervisor feedback loop
predictors.query_text_used: Structured social support and supervisor feedback loop | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Supportive context buffers fatigue and threat responses, improving performance consistency. | Intervention examples: Regular supervisor check-ins; peer support prompts; coaching. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Over the last 24 hours, rate how supported you felt by coworkers/supervisor (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Work_Education_and_Role_Functioning > Job_retention_and_support / energy_and_pacing_for_work_planning

mapping.rationale: The variable maps to a social support feedback loop in the workplace. The closest leaf is 794: 'feedback_conversation_support' under SOCIAL > work_education_and_role_functioning > work_and_occupational_solutions > workplace_relationships_and_team_functioning. The variable is measured via EMA self-report of perceived support from coworkers/supervisor over the past 24 hours (1-9), four times per day, aligning with a structured supervisor feedback loop. Mechanistically, structured social support buffers fatigue and threat responses, stabilizing performance. This leaf is the most specific operational construct matching P07's description and measurement item.

source_variable.label: Structured social support and supervisor feedback loop
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Supportive context buffers fatigue and threat responses, improving performance consistency.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Regular supervisor check-ins; peer support prompts; coaching.

measurement.item_or_signal: Over the last 24 hours, rate how supported you felt by coworkers/supervisor (1-9).
measurement.notes: Social support signal with supervisor feedback.

expected_effects.rationale: Perceived support reduces threat sensitivity.
Social support reduces fatigue burden.
Support may reduce error rate via improved focus.

feasibility.notes: Requires coordination with team; feasible with structured prompts
